agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO describes it at a high level with intentionally vague guardrails, leaving significant ambiguity in the spec.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand signals" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "A measure of current delivery request volume relative to capacity — the primary input signal for price adjustment" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme or irrational pricing outcomes" },
      { "term": "Base Price", "definition": "Implied but not stated — the reference price from which dynamic adjustments are made" },
      { "term": "Price Adjustment", "definition": "The delta applied to a delivery price in response to a demand signal, which can be positive (surge) or negative (discount)" },
      { "term": "Real Time", "definition": "Pricing changes are computed and applied to incoming orders as demand conditions change, not batched or scheduled" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to normal levels",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is the core business policy of DynaPricing. A VP could change the pricing strategy (e.g., switch to flat pricing during a PR crisis). It is a policy, not a data integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand exceeds the high-demand threshold, the computed price must be greater than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to normal levels",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Symmetric policy to BR-1. Leadership could disable discounting at any time to protect margins.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand falls below the low-demand threshold, the computed price must be less than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must be computed and applied in real time — not batched, delayed, or scheduled",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Real-time is a business expectation and SLA, not a physical constraint. The business could decide to batch-update prices every 5 minutes and the system would not be corrupt.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a change in demand conditions, the price offered to the next incoming order must reflect the updated demand level"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The system must operate fully automated with no manual intervention required to adjust prices",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is a business preference. A VP could mandate manual approval for price changes above a certain threshold.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a demand change event, price adjustment occurs without any human approval step in the pipeline"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Price guardrails must exist to prevent prices from reaching extreme values (both ceiling and floor)",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without guardrails, the system could produce $0.00 or $10,000 deliveries, which represents corrupt/irrational system state. A price of zero violates basic financial integrity. A runaway price causes reputational and possibly legal harm. The guardrails themselves are the invariant — the specific thresholds are policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "No computed delivery price may fall below the defined minimum price floor or exceed the defined maximum price ceiling"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "A delivery price must never be zero or negative",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative price means the company is paying customers to deliver packages. This is a financial integrity constraint — if violated, the system is producing corrupt outputs regardless of demand.",
      "testable_assertion": "For any demand level, the final computed price must be strictly greater than zero"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "The pricing relationship between demand and price must be monotonically non-decreasing — higher demand must never produce a lower price than lower demand, all else being equal",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If higher demand produced lower prices, the system would be logically incoherent and violate its own stated purpose. This is a consistency invariant — the pricing function must be well-ordered.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given two demand levels D1 > D2 with all other inputs equal, price(D1) >= price(D2)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The pricing engine must consider both competitiveness and profitability — neither objective may be completely sacrificed for the other",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "The balance between competitiveness and profitability is a strategic business decision that will shift over time (e.g., market-entry vs. mature-market phases).",
      "testable_assertion": "The dynamic price must not fall below cost (profitability floor) and must not exceed a competitive ceiling derived from market rates"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes to unprecedented levels (e.g., natural disaster, major event)", "expected_behavior": "Price ceiling guardrail activates — price caps at maximum regardless of demand signal. System may also need ethical/regulatory consideration for price gouging." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero", "expected_behavior": "Price floor guardrail activates — price cannot go below minimum. BR-6 ensures price stays positive." },
    { "scenario": "Demand oscillates rapidly (flapping)", "expected_behavior": "Undefined in spec. Risk of price instability causing customer confusion. Likely needs a dampening/smoothing mechanism." },
    { "scenario": "Demand signal is unavailable or corrupted", "expected_behavior": "Undefined in spec. System must have a fallback — likely revert to base price rather than compute from bad data." },
    { "scenario": "Two customers request a quote simultaneously at a demand boundary", "expected_behavior": "Undefined in spec. Must decide whether both get the same price or if the second request tips demand into the next tier." },
    { "scenario": "Price guardrail thresholds are misconfigured (e.g., floor > ceiling)", "expected_behavior": "System must reject the configuration and refuse to operate with contradictory guardrails. This is a system integrity issue." },
    { "scenario": "Competitive market rates drop below cost", "expected_behavior": "Tension between BR-8's competitiveness and profitability objectives. Profitability floor should win — the system should not sell below cost." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price gouging laws apply in certain jurisdictions", "expected_behavior": "Not addressed in spec. Dynamic pricing in logistics may be subject to local regulations, especially during emergencies." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing is a margin optimization lever. Upside: capture surplus during high demand. Downside: if guardrails fail, potential for revenue loss (prices too low) or customer churn (prices too high). Cost-to-serve must be a pricing floor.",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand signal infrastructure, pricing engine with low latency, and monitoring/alerting for guardrail breaches. Ops teams need visibility into pricing decisions for support escalations.",
    "user": "Customers will see variable prices, which creates transparency and trust concerns. Price volatility without explanation erodes confidence. UX must communicate why prices differ from expected norms.",
    "risk": "High risk in multiple dimensions: (1) Regulatory — price gouging laws in some jurisdictions, (2) Reputational — perception of exploitative pricing during crises, (3) Technical — a bug in the pricing engine could silently drain revenue or alienate customers at scale, (4) Competitive — if competitors offer flat pricing, dynamic pricing may be perceived negatively.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "See variable delivery prices; may pay more during peak times; may benefit from lower off-peak prices. Trust and transparency are critical." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue team", "impact": "Revenue forecasting becomes more complex with variable pricing. Need new reporting on price distribution, margin by demand tier." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will receive complaints about price variability. Need tooling to explain why a customer was charged a specific price at a specific time." },
      { "group": "Operations/Dispatch", "impact": "Demand signals must flow reliably to pricing engine. Capacity planning interacts with pricing — high prices may suppress demand, creating a feedback loop." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review price gouging regulations per jurisdiction. May need to disable dynamic pricing in certain regions or during declared emergencies." },
      { "group": "Sales/Partnerships", "impact": "Enterprise/contract customers may need fixed-price agreements exempt from dynamic pricing. B2B vs B2C pricing strategy divergence." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "The spec is a casual CEO statement from an all-hands meeting, not a product requirements document. It contains no quantitative thresholds, no defined demand signals, no guardrail parameters, no pricing formula, no mention of cost floors, no regulatory considerations, and explicitly hedges with 'probably' on guardrails. Nearly every critical parameter is undefined. The rules extracted are reasonable inferences but most lack explicit confirmation from the spec.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Price goes up when demand is high (explicitly stated)",
      "Price goes down when demand is low (explicitly stated)",
      "System is automated (explicitly stated)",
      "Some form of guardrails will exist (stated, though hedged with 'probably')"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Specific guardrail thresholds — not defined at all",
      "What 'demand' means concretely — order volume? capacity utilization? geographic density?",
      "What 'real time' means — sub-second? per-order? per-minute?",
      "Whether a base price exists and how it is determined",
      "How competitiveness is measured — against which competitors? what data source?",
      "Whether cost-of-delivery is a pricing floor (inferred, not stated)",
      "Whether there are customer segments exempt from dynamic pricing",
      "Regulatory and legal constraints on dynamic pricing",
      "How the pricing function works — linear? tiered? algorithmic?"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A base price exists from which adjustments are made (not stated)",
      "Demand refers to delivery request volume relative to available capacity",
      "Guardrails include both a price ceiling and a price floor",
      "Price must never go to zero or negative (basic financial integrity, not stated)",
      "Cost-of-delivery is a hard floor for profitability (inferred from 'profitable')",
      "The pricing function is monotonically non-decreasing with respect to demand (logical coherence)"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The CEO said 'probably' regarding guardrails. It is unclear whether guardrails are a firm requirement or aspirational. If guardrails are optional, BR-5 and BR-6 cannot be classified as invariants, and the system has no safety net against extreme prices.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Finance"],
      "options": ["A: Guardrails are mandatory and must ship with v1", "B: Guardrails are nice-to-have and can come in v2"],
      "recommendation": "A — guardrails are non-negotiable. Launching dynamic pricing without bounds is an unacceptable business risk."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No definition of 'demand' — is it order volume per hour, capacity utilization ratio, geographic request density, or something else? The entire pricing model depends on this signal definition.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Operations/Dispatch", "Data team"],
      "options": ["A: Demand = order volume per time window", "B: Demand = capacity utilization (orders / available couriers)", "C: Demand = composite signal (volume + geography + time)"],
      "recommendation": "B — capacity utilization is the most meaningful signal in logistics because raw volume without capacity context is misleading."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No pricing formula, thresholds, or quantitative parameters defined. Cannot write acceptance criteria without knowing: what is the price floor? price ceiling? demand tiers? adjustment percentages?",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Engineering", "Finance", "Product"],
      "options": ["A: Product team defines specific parameters before engineering begins", "B: Engineering builds a configurable framework and parameters are tuned later"],
      "recommendation": "A — parameters must be defined by Product/Finance first. Building without defined thresholds risks encoding wrong assumptions."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No mention of regulatory constraints. Dynamic pricing in logistics may be subject to price gouging laws in certain jurisdictions, especially during declared emergencies.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers", "Executive team"],
      "options": ["A: Legal review before launch", "B: Launch and address regulatory issues reactively"],
      "recommendation": "A — legal review is mandatory before launching dynamic pricing. Regulatory violations carry fines and reputational damage."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Tension between 'competitive' and 'profitable' is unresolved. When market rates drop below cost, which objective wins? This is a fundamental strategic question that the pricing model must encode.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Finance", "Sales/Partnerships", "Executive team"],
      "options": ["A: Profitability always wins — never sell below cost", "B: Competitiveness wins — match market even at a loss to retain volume", "C: Configurable per segment or time horizon"],
      "recommendation": "A — profitability floor should be the hard constraint. Competitiveness is a soft objective within profitable bounds."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
